what:
OpenFn adaptor for Salesforce. The adaptor lets OpenFn workflows read from and write to Salesforce Platform APIs (REST, SOAP, Bulk, Streaming).

It exposes 8 operations that you can call from a workflow step, listed in the Operations tab. 15 ready-to-paste snippets are grouped as create (7) and search (8). Vendor homepage: https://www.salesforce.com.
connect:
Salesforce supports OAuth 2.0 for API access.

The credential schema asks for loginUrl, username (required), password (required), securityToken, and apiVersion. An OAuth flow is wired up — use the OAuth schema on the Credentials tab to provision tokens through OpenFn rather than handling refresh yourself. Point the credential at the base URL that matches your environment: https://{instance}.salesforce.com/services/data/v60.0, https://{instance}.salesforce.com/services/Soap/u/60.0, https://{instance}.salesforce.com/services/async/60.0. Auth reference: https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=sf.remoteaccess_oauth_flows.htm.
when:
Reach for this adaptor when your workflow needs to read or write data in Salesforce Platform APIs (REST, SOAP, Bulk, Streaming).

9 triggers are catalogued (1 webhook, 8 polling), including New Attachment on Record, New Case Attachment, New Contact, New Field Update, and more. See the Triggers tab for the full list. Salesforce can push events to OpenFn via webhooks (docs: https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/platform/pub-sub-api/overview), so reactive workflows are a good fit.
wire:
A typical wiring pattern is trigger → fetch → transform → write.

Kick the workflow off from a trigger on the Triggers tab (webhook-driven where supported, polling otherwise). Then chain operations from this adaptor's Operations tab to fetch or write data in Salesforce. Pass values between steps through state.data — every operation reads from and writes to job state.

To move data between systems, follow this step with another adaptor (e.g. write the fetched records into a downstream FHIR, DHIS2, or database adaptor).
scale:
When running Salesforce jobs at volume:

- Prefer Salesforce webhooks over polling — they cut latency and rate-limit pressure.
- Batch requests where the API supports it and avoid fan-out loops over large collections.
- Make jobs idempotent by keying on stable external IDs so a replay doesn't create duplicates.
- Surface 4xx/5xx responses with the vendor request ID in your logs, and treat 429s as retries with backoff.

Adapter note: Multiple APIs: REST, SOAP (Partner/Enterprise WSDL), Bulk 2.0, Streaming/Pub-Sub, Tooling, Metadata.
best:
- Store Salesforce credentials in OpenFn's credential store; never inline keys or tokens in job code.
- Pin @openfn/language-salesforce to v9.0.8 in package.json so an upstream change doesn't break a production workflow silently.
- Keep each step small — one logical API call per operation makes failures easier to diagnose and retry.
- Add defensive checks before transforming inbound payloads — vendor responses change between versions.
- Log enough context (resource id, run id, vendor request id) so an incident can be traced end-to-end.
